\subsection{Ability Safety}
\label{sec:ability}

The most damaging ability bug is on |copy|.
A value whose type lacks |copy| is meant to be unique: this is what makes the |Capability| of Section~\ref{sec:type} a single-issue permission, and what makes a |Coin| balance impossible to counterfeit.
A runtime that produces a fresh value of a no-|copy| type silently duplicates that resource.

\subsubsection{Example}

While we do not know about a historical exploit around abilities, here is a simple imaginary example:

\begin{MoveBox}
fun clone(c: &Capability): Capability {
    *c   // ReadRef requires `copy` --- should be rejected.
}
\end{MoveBox}

The |Capability| type introduced in Section~\ref{sec:type} does not have the |copy| ability, thus the above code should be rejected. If for some reason the bytecode verifier does not do this, one can craft bytecode which silently creates a copy of the token.

\subsubsection{Performing the Checks}

When executing untrusted code, the VM performs checks for violations of each of the abilities |copy|, |drop|, |key|, and |store|.
Most of those checks are relatively straightforward and can be directly performed when the corresponding instruction is executed.
The exception is |drop|, which requires the VM to track which values have not been consumed on function exit.
